f35b89fb-3cc2-4e1f-8e58-98165c82df08.name: Notes
f35b89fb-3cc2-4e1f-8e58-98165c82df08.content:
Welcome to protoLabs Studio

This is your workspace notepad. Use it to jot down ideas, track decisions, or leave notes for your team.

Quick Start

  • Board — Create features, track progress, and manage your backlog
  • Agents — AI agents pick up features and implement them in isolated branches
  • Auto-Mode — Queue features with dependencies and let agents work autonomously
  • Context Files — Drop coding standards into .automaker/context/ to guide every agent

Useful Commands

If you're using the Claude Code plugin:

  • /board — View and manage the Kanban board
  • /auto-mode start — Start autonomous feature processing
  • /orchestrate — Set up feature dependencies
  • /plan-project — Plan a full project from an idea

Create new tabs to organize your notes by topic.

49ce8e0b-b2d9-43a9-8073-535698843379.name: Infra Update - Staging HTTPS
49ce8e0b-b2d9-43a9-8073-535698843379.content:
Staging Infra Update (2026-03-05)

PWA Install via Tailscale HTTPS

PWA install wasn't showing on staging because browsers require HTTPS. Staging was HTTP-only over Tailscale.

Changes

  • Tailscale HTTPS proxy added to setup-staging.sh — UI on :443, API on :8443. Runs automatically after health check on --start.

  • nginx fix — sw.js and manifest.webmanifest now served with no-cache + Service-Worker-Allowed: / instead of 7-day immutable cache.

  • PWA virtual module stub — Electron dev mode no longer errors on virtual:pwa-register/react.

Access

Use https://ava.<tailnet>.ts.net for PWA-eligible HTTPS on staging.

PRs

  • #1764 — PWA virtual module stub

  • #1765 — Tailscale HTTPS + nginx fix

adca04b8-ba97-466b-862a-0a04a43f5bfe.name: E2E Pipeline Architecture
adca04b8-ba97-466b-862a-0a04a43f5bfe.content:
protoLabs Studio: Idea-to-Production E2E Pipeline

Finalized 2026-03-06. This is the canonical reference for the full autonomous development lifecycle.

Overview

The pipeline has 7 phases. GTM is out of scope (separate initiative). Every phase is event-driven — completion of one phase triggers the next automatically.

SIGNAL INTAKE → IDEATION → PLANNING → EXECUTION → COMPLETION CASCADE → CEREMONIES → PROMOTION

Phase 1: Signal Intake

Service: SignalIntakeService

4 input channels: Discord, GitHub webhooks, MCP tools, UI

Classification: ops (board/agents/infra) | gtm (parked)

Output: Routed signal to appropriate handler

Entry Points

  • Discord suggestion → process_idea → PM Agent triage
  • MCP create_feature → direct to board (backlog)
  • MCP submit_prd → CoS pipeline → PM Agent
  • UI "New Feature" → direct to board (backlog)
  • Bug report → Discord #bug-reports → bug_triage channel workflow

Phase 2: Ideation & Intake

Single Feature Path

PM Agent Pipeline (authority-agents/pm-agent.ts):

triage(opus) → research(sonnet) → PRD(sonnet) → review → approval → feature on board

PM → ProjM handoff is implicit (PM approves, ProjM picks up if project-scale).

Project-Scale Path

Services: ProjectLifecycleService, ProjectOrchestrationService

Idea → Deep Research → SPARC PRD → Review → Approval
  → Milestone decomposition → Phase breakdown
  → ProjectOrchestrationService scaffolds:
    - Epic features (one per milestone)
    - Child features (one per phase)
    - Dependency wiring (topological)
    - Git branches (epic/* and feature/*)

Output: Features on board in BACKLOG with full dependency graph

Phase 3: Planning & Dependency Wiring

Service: DependencyResolverService

  • Topological sort produces execution order
  • Foundation features (isFoundation=true) block downstream until MERGED (not just review)
  • Epic hierarchy: feature/* → epic/* → dev
  • Auto-mode reads execution_order to pick next unblocked feature

Phase 4: Execution (Auto-Mode + Lead Engineer)

Services: AutoModeService, LeadEngineerService, LeadEngineerStateMachine

Auto-Mode Loop

while (backlog has unblocked features) {
  pick next feature by execution_order
  → LeadEngineerService.process(feature)
  wait for completion or failure
  handle result (advance or escalate)
}

LE State Machine

INTAKE → PLAN → EXECUTE → REVIEW → MERGE → DONE
   ↓        ↓       ↓        ↓       ↓
ESCALATE ←←←←←←←←←←←←←←←←←←←←←←←←←┘

Per State

  • INTAKE: Validate feature, check deps satisfied
  • PLAN: Agent generates implementation plan
  • EXECUTE: Agent works in isolated git worktree. Model routing: small→haiku, medium/large→sonnet, architectural→opus. Auto-escalation: 2+ failures → opus.
  • REVIEW: PR created, CI runs, CodeRabbit reviews. PR Maintainer agent handles formatting, thread resolution.
  • MERGE: Auto-merge when all checks pass
  • DONE: Feature complete, triggers completion cascade

14 Fast-Path Rules (pure functions, no LLM)

mergedNotDone, orphanedInProgress, staleDeps, autoModeHealth, staleReview, stuckAgent, capacityRestart, projectCompleting, prApproved, threadsBlocking, remediationStalled, classifiedRecovery, hitlFormResponse, missingCIChecks

Phase 5: Completion Cascade

Service: CompletionDetectorService (event-driven)

feature:done
  → all features in epic done? → epic:done
    → all epics in milestone done? → milestone:completed
      → all milestones in project done? → project:completed

Each level emits events consumed by CeremonySchedulerService.

Phase 6: Ceremonies

Services: CeremonyService, CeremonyStateMachine, CeremonySchedulerService

Ceremony State Machine

awaiting_kickoff → milestone_active → milestone_retro
  → milestone_active (next milestone) | project_retro → project_complete

Ceremony Types

  • Milestone kickoff: Start of work block. Context setting, priority alignment.
  • Milestone retro: LangGraph flow (collect → analyze → report). Reviews what shipped, what broke, lessons learned.
  • Project retro: Final retrospective across all milestones. Archives project.
  • Standup: On-demand status check. Not scheduled on a cadence — triggered by need.

Cadence

milestone kickoff → work (auto-mode) → milestone retro
  → next milestone kickoff → work → milestone retro
  → ... (repeat per milestone)
  → project retro → archive

Phase 7: Promotion Pipeline

Service: StagingPromotionService

dev → staging (Ava-autonomous)

list_staging_candidates
  → readiness check:
    1. CI passing on feature's dev merge commit
    2. No open CodeRabbit threads
    3. Feature marked done on board
    4. No held/rejected flags from previous batch
  → create_promotion_batch
  → cherry-pick onto promotion branch
  → PR into staging (--merge commit, NOT squash)
  → auto-merge

staging → main (HITL-gated)

promote_to_main
  → creates PR + HITL approval form
  → Human approves (form or GitHub)
  → Merge (--merge commit)
  → Tag release
  → Deploy notification to #deployments

Ava NEVER merges staging→main herself.

Observability & Traceability

  • EventLedgerService: Append-only JSONL (events.jsonl). 13 event types: feature.*, agent.*, pr.*, build.*, promotion.*
  • LedgerService: Per-feature-completion metrics (metrics.jsonl). Duration, attempts, model, lines changed.
  • ArchivalService: On project:completed — move to .automaker/archive/, stub at original path.
  • Langfuse: Trace analysis and cost tracking per agent session.
  • TrajectoryStore: Verified execution trajectories for learning flywheel.

Git Flow

feature/* → epic/* (if in epic) → dev → staging → main
                  ↓                       ↓
              (squash OK)          (merge commit ONLY)
  • Feature PRs: target epic branch (if epicId) or dev. Squash allowed.
  • Epic PRs: target dev. Merge commit.
  • dev → staging: Cherry-pick promotion. Merge commit.
  • staging → main: Direct PR. Merge commit. HITL-gated.

Known Gaps (as of 2026-03-06)

  1. Promotion uses --squash (FIXED — now --merge)
  2. No CI enforcement that staging PRs come from dev — could bypass dev QA
  3. Ceremony scheduler fragile on restart — timers lost, state persists but re-hydration is best-effort
  4. CompletionDetector dedup is in-memory — restart could re-fire completion events
  5. Dual ledger system — EventLedger and LedgerService overlap; consider consolidating

c31961f0-7b59-452f-b46f-816c014f4a5b.name: Hardware Upgrade Plan
c31961f0-7b59-452f-b46f-816c014f4a5b.content:
Hardware Upgrade Plan

Drafted 2026-03-06

Final Machine Layout

MachineCPU / BoardGPURAMPSUOSRole
AM5 Inference RigRyzen 9 7950X3D / ROG Crosshair X670E Extreme2x RTX PRO 6000 Blackwell (96GB each, 192GB total)64GB DDR5-60001600WWindows 11 / Ubuntu 24.04 LTS (dual boot, separate NVMe)Primary inference (big models), gaming
AM4 ServerRyzen 9 5900X / ROG STRIX X570-ERTX A6000 Ada (48GB)128GB DDR4-36001000WUbuntu 24.04 LTS ServerDocker Swarm, services, smaller models, image gen
Z170 Proxmoxi7-6700 / ASUS Z170-AGTX 107032GB DDR4850WProxmoxVMs, lightweight services

New Hardware to Purchase

  • 2nd RTX PRO 6000 Blackwell
  • 1600W PSU (for AM5 rig)
  • 1TB PCIe 5.0 NVMe SSD (~15GB/s) — model storage, goes in M.2_1 slot on X670E for full Gen 5 speed

Hardware to Sell

  • RTX 4090 TUF — no longer needed, Blackwell handles gaming + inference

Key Technical Notes

AM5 PCIe Lane Layout (X670E Extreme)

  • Single GPU: PCIe 5.0 x16 — full bandwidth
  • Dual GPU: PCIe 5.0 x8/x8 — still 32GB/s each (= PCIe 4.0 x16)
  • M.2_1: PCIe 5.0 x4 (no lane conflict with single GPU)
  • M.2_2: Using this drops GPU slot 1 to x8
  • Put Gen 5 SSD in M.2_1 only

Dual Boot Setup (AM5)

  • Samsung 980 Pro 1TB → Windows 11
  • WD Black SN850X 1TB → Ubuntu 24.04 LTS
  • New Gen 5 1TB SSD → Model storage (ext4 or NTFS if shared)
  • Install each OS with other drive disconnected — separate EFI partitions
  • Switch via BIOS boot menu (F8) — no shared bootloader

GPU Power Management

  • Blackwell cards: power limit to ~500W each for inference (saves ~200W total, ~5-10% throughput loss)
  • sudo nvidia-smi -i 0 -pl 500 / sudo nvidia-smi -i 1 -pl 500
  • Set up systemd service for persistence on boot
  • ⚠️ Watch for known thermal throttle bug on RTX PRO 6000 Blackwell — monitor with driver updates

NVIDIA Driver

  • Blackwell requires open kernel modules (nvidia-driver-570-open or newer)
  • Proprietary modules will load but fail with "requires use of NVIDIA open kernel modules"
  • Use graphics-drivers PPA for latest driver versions

Capacity After Upgrade

  • Inference VRAM: 192GB (2x Blackwell) + 48GB (Ada) = 240GB total across fleet
  • System RAM: 64GB + 128GB + 32GB = 224GB total
  • CPU cores: 16 + 12 + 4 = 32 cores total

a9b74a8e-32c1-4ad6-9e56-967a0b4a5a4d.name: CRDT Sync - Due Diligence
a9b74a8e-32c1-4ad6-9e56-967a0b4a5a4d.content:
Distributed CRDT Sync - Due Diligence Report

Date: 2026-03-06 | Status: Approved for project planning

Vision

Multiple Automaker instances (Josh-dev, Josh-staging, Josh-prod, collaborator nodes) share board state in real-time over Tailscale VPN. Each instance runs its own agent team but sees and acts on a unified board. Features flow to available compute via work-stealing. Offline-first — every instance works independently and merges seamlessly on reconnect.

Shared Domains

DomainData ShapeWrite FrequencyConflict ProfileCRDT Strategy
FeaturesFlat JSON, ~30 fieldsVery highHIGHLWW-Map per field
ProjectsNested JSON + markdownLow-mediumMediumLWW-Map, text CRDT for MD
NotesRich text (Tiptap HTML)MediumMediumLWW initially, @automerge/prosemirror later
Shared SettingsJSON configRareLowLWW-Map with layered merge

NOT Shared (Instance-Local)

  • Agent output (agent-output.md, raw-output.jsonl) — streaming, single-writer
  • Trajectories — append-only, instance-local
  • Context files — user-curated per instance (syncs via git)
  • Memory files — agent learnings (syncs via git)
  • Credentials — never shared
  • Git worktrees — local to each machine

Technology Evaluation

Automerge 3.0 (RECOMMENDED)

  • JSON ergonomics: A+ — native JSON data model, doc.features[id].status = "review" just works
  • Memory: A — 10-100x improvement in v3 (Moby Dick: 700MB to 1.3MB)
  • Sync: A — automerge-repo handles peer management, WebSocket adapter, persistence
  • Text: B+ — @automerge/prosemirror official package for ProseMirror/Tiptap
  • Multi-language: A — Rust core, JS/WASM, Python, C bindings

Yjs (Strong alternative, not chosen)

  • Tiptap integration: A+ — native via @tiptap/extension-collaboration
  • JSON ergonomics: B — Y.Map/Y.Array require explicit type wrapping
  • Ecosystem: A — largest CRDT ecosystem
  • Why not: 90% of sync surface is structured JSON where Automerge dominates. Yjs wins on Tiptap integration but that is a Phase 5 concern.

Eliminated Options

  • Loro — fastest benchmarks but no built-in sync protocol. Watch list.
  • cr-sqlite/sqlite-sync — cr-sqlite not production ready; sqlite-sync Elastic License 2.0 restricts production use
  • Custom LWW-Map — reinventing 8+ years of Automerge work. No.
  • Git-based sync — too slow (1-5s) for operational state. Keep for knowledge layer only.

Architecture

Sync Topology: Star with Leader Election

One instance (role: primary) runs automerge-repo-sync-server. Others connect as WebSocket clients. If primary goes down, next instance by config order promotes. Tailscale provides stable IPs and encrypted transport.

Josh-Dev (primary + hub) <--> Josh-Staging (worker) <--> Josh-Prod (readonly)
         |                            |                          |
         +------- automerge-repo sync (WebSocket over Tailscale) ----+
         |
   Shared Automerge Documents:
   - doc:features    (Record<id, Feature>)
   - doc:projects    (Record<slug, Project>)
   - doc:notes       (NotesWorkspace)
   - doc:config      (SharedSettings)
   - doc:assignments (instance <-> feature map)

proto.config.yaml (Layered Config)

# git-tracked, shared across all instances
hive:
  id: "protolabs-ava"
  syncPort: 3009
  heartbeatMs: 30000

defaults:
  maxConcurrency: 6
  defaultModel: "sonnet"
  gitWorkflow:
    prBaseBranch: "dev"

instances:
  josh-dev:
    role: primary
    url: "100.x.x.x"
    domains: ["apps/ui/", "libs/"]
  josh-staging:
    role: worker
    url: "100.x.x.y"
    domains: ["apps/server/"]
  josh-prod:
    role: readonly
    domains: []

assignment:
  strategy: "capacity"   # capacity | domain | manual
  stealThreshold: 0      # steal when own backlog hits 0
  stealMax: 3            # max features to steal per cycle

Resolution order: proto.config.yaml defaults < shared CRDT settings < .automaker/settings.json (local) < environment variables

Cross-Instance Feature Assignment

New Feature fields: assignedInstance, instanceOrigin, stealable

Work-stealing protocol:

  1. Instance A's backlog empties
  2. A broadcasts WORK_REQUEST via CRDT ephemeral messages
  3. Peers respond with WORK_OFFER (stealable features matching A's domains)
  4. A picks features, sets assignedInstance = A (CRDT field update, propagates to all)
  5. A's auto-mode picks up newly assigned features

Simpler than classical work-stealing: coarse granularity (hours not microseconds), low contention (~10s of features), CRDT handles assignment without distributed locking.

Codebase Seams (Already Prepared)

  • FeatureStore interface (libs/types/src/feature-store.ts) — pluggable storage with claim()/release(), designed for CRDT swap
  • EventBus.broadcast() (apps/server/src/lib/events.ts) — comment: "In hivemind mode, this will also publish to the mesh"
  • HivemindConfig types (libs/types/src/hivemind.ts) — InstanceIdentity, HivemindPeer, HivemindDomain, capacity metrics
  • instance-state.md (docs/dev/instance-state.md) — documents shared vs local split, hivemind phases 0-4

Implementation Phases

M1: Foundation

  1. proto.config Loader (medium) — YAML schema, layered merge, instance identity
  2. libs/crdt Package (large) — Automerge 3 + automerge-repo wrapper, CRDTStore, WebSocket sync, persistence
  3. Sync Server + Leader Election (large) — sync lifecycle, heartbeat, failover

M2: Feature Sync

  1. AutomergeFeatureStore (large) — FeatureStore interface backed by CRDT, fallback to FeatureLoader
  2. EventBus CRDT Bridge (medium) — remote changes trigger local events, no duplicates

M3: Projects, Notes, Settings

  1. Project Sync (medium) — same pattern as features
  2. Notes Workspace Sync (medium) — workspace-level LWW, not character-level yet
  3. Shared Settings Sync (medium) — layered resolution with local overrides

M4: Cross-Instance Assignment

  1. Capacity Advertising (medium) — metrics in CRDT doc
  2. Work-Stealing Protocol (large) — WORK_REQUEST/OFFER, domain routing, assignedInstance
  3. Unified Dashboard (large) — instance badges, peers panel, cross-instance view

M5: Rich Text and Polish

  1. Notes Rich-Text CRDT (large) — @automerge/prosemirror + Tiptap integration
  2. Operational Hardening (medium) — compaction, diagnostics, reconnection, docs

Key Decisions

DecisionRationale
Automerge over Yjs90% of sync surface is structured JSON. Automerge's native JSON model eliminates Y.Map/Y.Array boilerplate.
Star over mesh topology2-5 nodes on Tailscale. Star is simpler, mesh adds O(n^2) connections for no benefit at this scale.
proto.config.yaml in gitInstance registry and shared defaults should be version-controlled. Local overrides stay in .automaker/settings.json.
Work-stealing over central schedulerCRDT-native approach. No single point of failure. Assignment is a field update, not a coordination protocol.
Notes text CRDT deferred to M5Workspace-level LWW sync covers 90% of note sharing needs. Character-level co-editing is a polish feature.
Knowledge layer stays on gitContext/memory/skills have different write patterns (rare, human-curated). Git sync is already working.

Sources

  • Automerge 3.0 announcement
  • automerge-repo networking docs
  • @automerge/prosemirror npm
  • automerge-repo-sync-server
  • Tiptap collaboration (Yjs)
  • Loro CRDT
  • cr-sqlite
  • Tailscale architecture

M6: Ava Orchestrator Mesh

Added post-initial planning. This is the coordination intelligence layer that makes the hivemind actually smart.

Concept

Each Automaker instance runs an Ava orchestrator (Claude Code session with the /ava skill). Today these Avas are isolated — they manage their own board but have no visibility into what other instances are doing. The Ava Mesh gives them a shared communication channel.

What Ava Instances Can Do

Message TypeExampleTrigger
status-update"Josh-dev: 3 features in progress, 2 in review, backlog empty. All agents healthy."Periodic (every 15 min) + on feature completion
help-request"Josh-staging: Feature X is blocked — needs frontend expertise. Can any instance with UI domain knowledge pick this up?"On escalation or domain mismatch
help-response"Josh-dev: I have UI domain ownership and capacity. Accepting Feature X."In response to help-request
work-handoff"Josh-dev: Handing off Feature Y to Josh-staging — it modifies server routes, better fit for your domain."Domain-aware reassignment
escalation"Josh-prod: Agent has failed 3 times on Feature Z. Requesting operator attention."Failure threshold exceeded
coordination"Josh-dev: I'm about to merge a types change that affects Feature W on Josh-staging. Heads up — you may need to rebase."Cross-cutting changes detected
heartbeat"Josh-staging: Online, 6/8 agents active, 45% memory."Every heartbeat interval

Cadence Options

  • Periodic check-in — configurable in proto.config.yaml (default: 15 minutes). Ava posts a status-update summarizing board state, capacity, and any blockers.
  • Event-driven — on feature completion, escalation, backlog empty, or agent failure. More responsive, no polling waste.
  • On-demand — human operator or Ava skill invokes MCP tool directly.
  • Recommended: Event-driven + periodic heartbeat. Most coordination happens around events (feature done, blocked, idle). Periodic heartbeat catches silent failures.

Data Model

interface AvaMeshMessage {
  id: string;                    // unique message ID
  instanceId: string;            // sender's instanceId
  instanceName: string;          // human-readable (e.g. "Josh-dev")
  recipient: string | 'broadcast'; // target instance or all
  messageType: 'status-update' | 'help-request' | 'help-response' 
             | 'work-handoff' | 'escalation' | 'coordination' | 'heartbeat';
  content: string;               // human-readable message
  context?: {                    // structured metadata
    featureId?: string;
    featureTitle?: string;
    boardSummary?: BoardSummary;
    capacity?: InstanceCapacity;
    error?: string;
  };
  timestamp: string;             // ISO timestamp
  threadId?: string;             // groups request + response
}

CRDT Document: doc:ava-mesh

Append-only list (Automerge list CRDT). Messages are never edited or deleted — the log is immutable history. Compaction can archive old messages periodically (e.g., keep last 7 days in active doc, archive to disk).

MCP Tools

  • send_mesh_message — send typed message to peer or broadcast
  • list_mesh_messages — query recent messages with filters (sender, type, time range)
  • request_peer_help — structured help request with feature context
  • broadcast_status — send board summary + capacity to all peers

UI: Ava Mesh View

New sidebar view showing the inter-Ava conversation log. Features:

  • Chronological message display with instance badges
  • Color-coded message types (blue=status, amber=help, red=escalation, green=handoff)
  • Thread grouping (request + response linked)
  • Real-time updates via CRDT sync
  • Filter by instance, type, or time range
  • Human operator can inject messages (override/direct Ava instances)

Integration with Ava Skill

The /ava skill prompt gets updated when hivemind is active:

  • On activation: check mesh for recent messages, respond to pending help-requests
  • On feature completion: broadcast status-update
  • On escalation: send escalation message to mesh before alerting human
  • On idle: check peer capacity, consider requesting work via work-stealing protocol
  • Periodic: post heartbeat with board summary

Example Conversation Flow

[15:00] Josh-dev (Ava): STATUS — 2 features in_progress (auth-service, rate-limiter), 
        1 in review (user-profile). Backlog empty. 4/6 agents idle.

[15:01] Josh-staging (Ava): STATUS — 5 features in backlog, 6/6 agents active. 
        Running hot — 82% memory.

[15:01] Josh-dev (Ava): HELP-RESPONSE — I have capacity and backend domain ownership. 
        Requesting 2 features from Josh-staging via work-stealing.

[15:02] Josh-staging (Ava): WORK-HANDOFF — Transferring "api-rate-limiting" and 
        "webhook-retry-logic" to Josh-dev. Both are backend, medium complexity.

[15:45] Josh-dev (Ava): COORDINATION — Merging types change in libs/types that adds 
        RateLimitConfig. Josh-staging: your "api-throttle" feature may need rebase.

[15:46] Josh-staging (Ava): COORDINATION — Acknowledged. Will rebase api-throttle 
        after your PR merges to dev.

[16:00] Josh-prod (Ava): ESCALATION — Feature "payment-webhook" has failed 3 times. 
        Error: Stripe API key not configured in this instance. Needs operator attention.
